data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Wall Street ended lower on the week as elevated U.S.-Iran tensions remained in focus—with officials signaling the conflict could extend several more weeks—while President Donald Trump unveiled a high-profile tech advisory panel and Treasury yields surged to multi-month highs.

Geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and Iran remained elevated, with the conflict continuing to dominate market focus. Secretary of State Marco Rubio indicated to G7 foreign ministers that hostilities are expected to persist for another two to four weeks, suggesting the administration sees a potential end to the conflict within a relatively near-term window.

President Donald Trump has tapped several of the most prominent tech figures in business to serve on a technology advisory panel, including Meta Platforms (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Oracle (ORCL) Executive Chairman Larry Ellison, and Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang, to help shape the administration’s approach to science and technology.

U.S. Treasury yields surged this week, with benchmark rates hitting multi-month highs and capturing global investor attention. The 10-year climbed to 4.48%—its highest since July 2025—while the 2-year and 30-year also reached their strongest levels in months, reflecting shifting expectations around monetary policy.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) lost -2.1%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) slid -3.2%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell -0.9%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

Boomer economy

This week saw a high-profile Senate hearing on the looming social security crisis as the Boomer generation settles deep into retirement. At the same time, Boomers continue to hold most wealth in the U.S., while making up just 20% of the country's population. Those demographics and coming changes will impact certain sectors of the economy, as well as shape stock themes in the years to come. Read more.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

Chewy (CHWY) Is Using AI To Become Much More Profitable.

Buy Disney (DIS) When Valuation Is Low And Crude Oil High.

Take-Two (TTWO): What The Market Is Missing About GTA VI.

Boston Scientific (BSX): Under Pressure, But Fortunes May Shift.

Centene's (CNC) Earnings Reset Opens The Door For Re-Rating.

Blue Owl (OWL) Looks Incredibly Cheap. That's Not A Compliment.

Perfection Is A Fragile Thing To Hold - Sandisk (SNDK) Is A Sell.

Time To Harvest Gains And Move On From KVH Industries (KVHI).

BIZD Vs. PBDC: I'm Downgrading To 'Sell' As Yield Trap Closes.

Super Micro (SMCI) Has Lots Of Smoke: Watch Out For The Fire.

Weekly Movement

U.S. Indices
Dow -0.9% to 45,167. S&P 500 -2.1% to 6,369. Nasdaq -3.2% to 20,948. Russell 2000 +0.5% to 2,450. CBOE Volatility Index +15.9% to 31.05.

Top S&P 500 Gainers

data.title: Klarna: Buy Now, Or Pay Later
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The previously highly valued private fintech company Klarna Group plc (KLAR) went public in 2025, and the initial market reaction could not be described as pleasant for pre-IPO shareholders. However, the significant plummet in Klarna's stock since its IPO presents an attractive risk-versus-reward case at the current valuation. The investment thesis consists of three core pillars, most of which the market currently ignores despite multiple data points already showing Klarna's ability to act on these opportunities.

Firstly, Klarna operates in 26 markets, representing $19 trillion in retail spending. Even when limiting the market potential to Klarna's already existing TAM, Klarna's take rate of 2.8% as of Q4 2025, implies $532 billion of potential revenue. This highlights how huge Klarna's TAM is, as only small market share increases are equivalent to explosive growth. Secondly, Klarna is on a mission to not only add new users but also to convert current basic BNPL customers to banking customers. Succeeding in transforming only a fraction of their 118 million active users would explode Klarna's ARPAC due to substantially higher revenue per banking user. Lastly, I believe Klarna has yet to show its true operating leverage, as the company will continue to implement AI in its mission to become even more efficient. Klarna's current AI implementation has already exploded revenue per employee by reducing headcount, which in theory funnels future revenue straight to the bottom line.

In addition to previous points, the market is currently punishing Klarna for what many would consider the result of a conservative accounting method, which heavily impacted Klarna's margins. As the market starts to view Klarna's path to profitability as more achievable and robust, as well as move past the recent accounting illusion depicted in Klarna's Q4, the stock should start its path towards IPO levels yet again.

Understanding the Business Model

Klarna's overall strategy could be divided into two separate phases. The first phase was the main focus for many years and implied a focus on scaling its global transaction network. This phase is now largely complete with 118 million active users and over one million merchants, allowing Klarna to focus more on its next phase. Klarna's substantial global transaction network, allows for its next phase of converting existing customers into full banking customers. This is done by offering credit cards, savings accounts, financing products, etc. Achieving success in Klarna's second phase would be extremely profitable for Klarna, as both revenue and margins per customer would experience explosive growth.

Making Money the Klarna Way

Although the market's belief that Klarna's revenue model is vulnerable to economic downturns is true, I believe the market doesn't give Klarna enough credit for being more diversified than its closest competitors. Klarna's revenue consists of three revenue streams: merchant, consumer, and advertising revenue.

  • The most significant revenue stream is merchant revenue, which derives from merchants paying Klarna fees for offering their customers flexible financing alternatives, leading to higher order value. This segment reached $743 million in Q4 2025, increasing by 24% from the year prior.
  • Secondly, consumer revenue represents a substantial part of Klarna's total revenue, although considerably less than for most competitors, including Affirm (AFRM). This segment includes interest income and is Klarna's fastest-growing revenue stream. As of Q4 2025, interest income increased by 47% to $267 million, driven by Fair Financing GMV increasing 165% YoY to $4.5 billion. However, this growth engine entails suppression of margins in the short-term, as accounting standards force Klarna to provision for credit losses upfront. In other words, explosive growth within this segment will indirectly cause a significant but temporary "artificial" impact on Klarna's margins.
  • Lastly, with 55 million MAU, Klarna monetizes its users through digital advertising and affiliate marketing. This represents a small part of Klarna's revenue and will most likely stay that way, but it is important to note that this value proposition only gets more valuable for merchants as Klarna's global network grows, hence having the potential to grow.

The Elephant in the Room

Klarna's recent Q4 results displayed strong revenue growth at 38% YoY, reaching $1 billion in quarterly revenue for the first time and beating its guidance. Furthermore, GMV increased 32% YoY to $38.7 billion, and active users exceeded 118 million, growing 28% from the year prior. In addition to this, Klarna reported promising numbers for its newer banking offering, with 15.8 million banking customers, representing an increase of 101% for the new and highly profitable segment.

However, this was unfortunately not enough to satisfy investors, as the market was more focused on Klarna's profit margins and, more specifically, a perceived miss on its transaction margin dollars (TMD). Klarna's TMD reached $622 million before provisions and $372 million after, compared to its guidance of $390 million, representing a 31% and 17% YoY increase, respectively. Since TMD displays Klarna's underlying credit performance, it acts as a core operating metric for Klarna. The market being alarmed by the miss in TMD would therefore be reasonable if it were not for a specific factor that makes these results misleading. Hence, the TMD before provisions number more accurately displays Klarna's true credit performance, which is far less concerning.

By following current accounting standards, Klarna is forced to book the provision for expected credit losses upfront when a loan is originated and only recognizes the interest revenue gradually over the loan's lifespan. This implies that every new loan Klarna originates, is unprofitable from an accounting view in the beginning and gradually becomes profitable as interest payments are paid. Since Klarna originated a massive volume of longer-term loans in Q4, this entailed a substantial upfront hit to margins, suppressing the underlying profitability of these loans. Hence, this miss could partly be explained as a result of Klarna managing to originate a significantly greater volume of loans.

Moreover, Klarna's credit performance shows stability as the charge-off rate for its riskier Fair Financing products fluctuates between 3% and 4%. Additionally, the blended average across all products is relatively low at 1.1% for banking and 0.6% for basic users. These numbers are closely aligned with standard industry averages, disproving the narrative of Klarna's credit quality deteriorating.

Path to Profitability

While the market seems concerned with Klarna's negative operating cash flow, it should only be viewed as natural for a growing bank with the mission to aggressively expand its loan book. Hence, the negative operating cash flow should not be perceived as alarming for Klarna's continued path towards profitability. Instead, multiple factors support Klarna achieving its long-term profitability potential.

Firstly, continued user base expansion and higher adoption of Klarna's banking offering would significantly boost revenue and margins. The metrics highlighted in the table below, clearly indicate the highly profitable conversion of transforming basic users into banking users. As of Q4 data, banking customers entail $107 in ARPU compared to the significantly lower $30 ARPU of a basic user. Hence, succeeding in continued adoption of Klarna's banking offering would have a substantial impact on Klarna's profitability.

The recent Q4 results already show great promise of converting users, demonstrating proof of their strategy. Despite growing 101% YoY, this segment remains relatively small at only 13% of the total user base. This speaks for continued potential to convert more users, and converting just an additional 10% of existing users would increase ARPU by around 25%. Moreover, this assumption ignores the potential of new banking products as well as continued user base expansion.

The explosive growth rate in active card users, consumer deposits, and banking consumers confirms that Klarna plays a bigger role for its users now than before. This supports Klarna's mission of becoming a more complete banking service for its customers. However, these deposits are also beneficial for another reason, since they provide Klarna with a lower-cost funding base compared to peers lacking the same structure. This enables wider net interest margins as the loan book continues to expand, which in turn contributes to the segment's profitability.

Lastly, Klarna's efficiency measures have already enabled the company to rapidly expand while simultaneously decreasing its total headcount, causing its revenue per employee to skyrocket by more than 250% since 2022 (see graph below). Additionally, adjusted operating expenses have decreased by 8% since 2022, while Klarna's revenue doubled, highlighting its impressive operating leverage. For 2026, management is guiding for at least 6.9% in adjusted operating margins, which should be more than achievable. Furthermore, once the hyper-growth within the Fair Financing products segment starts to slow down and stabilize, the deferred revenue will catch up with the upfront provisions, leading to rapid margin expansion.

Valuation

When comparing Klarna in relation to its closest competitor, Affirm, Klarna stands out as attractive. As displayed above, the revenue mix differs between the companies, as Affirm is more reliant on consumer fees and less diversified than Klarna. Consequently, Affirm is more sensitive to the cost of capital in the financial markets. This can be put in contrast to Klarna, which possesses an EU banking license and is experiencing rapidly growing consumer deposits. Therefore, Klarna should be more resilient to recessions and interest rate shocks, due to its revenue mix and more stable funding base. Although it is important to note that these superior characteristics are far from enough to change Klarna's sensitive nature to macroeconomic changes. Despite these advantages, Klarna currently trades at a discount to Affirm based on several forward-looking metrics, including EV/Sales.

However, Klarna's unique capital and business structure make it hard to conduct an effective peer valuation. Evaluating Klarna from a broader perspective, the stock seems cheap at a P/B ratio just under 2. This can be compared to US industry averages for banks of P/B at 1.62 and 3.66 for financial services excluding banks and insurance. This puts Klarna's P/B valuation above the industry average for banks, although below some banks such as JPMorgan. To value Klarna closely in line with banks growing at a CAGR around 4% is absurd, given Klarna could achieve growth north of 20%-25% for the next five years.

To further understand Klarna's potential within the next 5 years, three scenarios characterized by different growth, margins, and dilution are modeled in the table below. In a base case scenario, Klarna grows revenue at a 20% CAGR to $8.7 billion, with 12.5%, or $1.09 billion, reaching the bottom line. Assuming 12.5% dilution during the period and a reasonable P/E exit multiple of 20, it would imply a share price of $51 in 2030. There is a relatively probable outcome of Klarna exceeding these targets if they successfully execute converting users to banking users. Hence, in a bull case scenario, Klarna could achieve 25% CAGR and 15% net income margin, representing $10.7 billion in revenue and $1.6 billion in net income. In this case of higher profitability, Klarna would be able to counter a lot of stock-based compensation, leading to lower dilution. When assuming 10% dilution and a higher P/E multiple of 25 for this scenario, it equates to $96 a share in 2030. However, even when considering a bear case of 15% revenue CAGR, 10% net income margin, 15% dilution, and a 15x multiple, it's hard to imagine significant downside from here with an implied share price of $24. This implies substantial upside potential with a considerable margin of safety, as Klarna can miss these targets and still deliver substantial returns from current levels around $13.

Scenario Bear Case Base Case Bull Case
Revenue Growth 15% 20% 25%
Est. Revenue 2030 $7 Billion $8.7 Billion $10.7 Billion
Est. Net Income Margin 10% 12.5% 15%
Est. Net Income $0.7 Billion $1.09 Billion $1.6 Billion
P/E Exit Multiple 15x 20x 25x
Dilution 15% 12.5% 10%
Share Count 434 Million 424 Million 415 Million
Implied Share Price $24 $51 $96

Key Risks

However, there are three main risks investors should monitor closely that potentially could endanger the investment thesis:

  • Firstly, Klarna's dependency and exposure to credit risks make the company vulnerable to macroeconomic shifts. It's therefore crucial for Klarna and similar businesses to maintain sufficient risk management when originating loans. If a company fails to maintain sufficient risk management for its loan origination, a recession will quickly expose these vulnerabilities, or as the Buffett saying goes, "Only when the tide goes out do you discover who's been swimming naked".
  • Secondly, the finance sector is heavily scrutinized and regularly faces regulatory challenges, which is even more accurate for BNPL and consumer credit providers like Klarna. For instance, sudden changes in underwriting mandates can cause severe consequences for Klarna's Fair Financing segment. On the other hand, the regulatory landscape surrounding the finance market, also acts as a barrier to entry for new actors trying to enter the market.
  • Lastly, the rapidly growing $400 billion fintech market is not only good news for Klarna, as it attracts large and powerful competitors to the market. Hence, to survive within the competitive market that is fintech, companies need to be agile and aggressive. PayPal can be interpreted as a recent and clear case study on what can happen when a company fails to keep up with the competitive landscape of fintech. From a theoretical perspective, increased competition could increase Klarna's CAC and decrease its take rate. However, significant competition within the fintech market is nothing new, and Klarna's take rate has actually increased from 2.3% in 2022 to 2.7% for fiscal 2025. This shows Klarna's competitive strengths, as it historically has managed to successfully compete within the competitive sector.

The Bottom Line

Following its significant stock price plummet since going public, Klarna is finally at an intriguing valuation. The market is currently punishing Klarna for its Q4 transaction margin miss, although this miss merely represents the consequences of conservative accounting practices, driven by explosive growth in its Fair Financing segment.

Underneath all the noise is a dominant and highly efficient global payment network, with an impressive growth engine and operating leverage. With over 118 million active users and growing, the conversion of basic users to banking users remains a largely untapped and substantial growth opportunity for Klarna in the coming years.

These factors, combined with an attractive valuation, make Klarna one of the most compelling investment cases within the fintech sector as of today. Not surprisingly, I am not alone in this belief, as the CEO and co-founder increased his ownership to 7.49% just before the IPO. Moreover, Klarna Chairman Michael Moritz recently bought $50 million worth of shares following the stock's significant pullback, displaying considerable conviction among insiders.

data.title: Weekly Commentary: Lacking A Good Scenario
data.description:
The problem, as I see it, is that fragile markets have more to lose with each passing day. And with each day of intense bombing, there is seemingly less to lose for the IRGC. As goes the markets, so go global economic prospects. Four weeks in, the likelihood of precarious escalation remains on a steady upward trajectory. Markets Friday began to accept the seriousness of an unfolding predicament not readily amenable to Trump and Fed "puts." With reliable backstops uncertain, markets must begin contemplating precarious scenarios for the first time in a while.

The Bloomberg MAG7 Index slumped 2.8% Friday, boosting the week's loss to 4.9% - with the index closing the week at the lowest level since August 1st (down 15.7% y-t-d). Friday's losses included Meta Platforms down 4.0%, Amazon 4.0%, Tesla 2.8%, and Microsoft 2.5%. For the week, Meta sank 11.4%, Alphabet 8.9%, Microsoft 6.6%, Nvidia 3.0% and Amazon 2.9%. Meta ended the week at the lowest close since April 23rd, Microsoft April 8th, Nvidia September 5th, and Alphabet November 28th. Amazon closed the week within 55 cents of the low back to May 9th. Air is leaking from the AI Bubble.

March 24 - New York Times (Maureen Farrell): "The private credit industry, a once-booming corner of the lending markets, is facing an investor exodus and heightened scrutiny of its risky practices. The bad news seems to keep coming. On Tuesday, Ares Management, an investment firm with more than $600 billion in assets, said it would block roughly half of the redemption requests from one of its private credit funds. A day earlier, the private equity giant Apollo Global Management said the same of one of its funds… Also on Monday, the debt-ratings agency Moody's downgraded a private credit fund run by KKR to junk status, warning investors that a growing number of the fund's outstanding loans were not being repaid and that its profitability is at risk."

March 26 - Bloomberg (Olivia Fishlow and Ellen DiMauro): "A wave of redemption requests across the private credit industry has left more than $4.6 billion of investor capital trapped behind withdrawal limits, with more asset managers expected to impose curbs in the coming weeks. Investors have looked to pull roughly $13 billion from over a dozen funds so far this quarter… But since the vehicles can cap withdrawals at 5% of net assets per quarter, investors have only been able to access about two-thirds of the cash they've sought… How much investors seek to cash out, and whether the firm again opts to avoid imposing limits, will be a key to gauging pressure on the sector, market participants say."

The crisis of confidence and run on "private Credit" continues to gain momentum. The Iran War certainly accelerated the process. At this point, however, even a rapid end to the conflict would likely not reverse the exodus. The reversal of flows has already revealed fundamental flaws and serious weaknesses in "private" high-risk lending. Historic lending and speculative cycles have largely run their courses. Importantly, the thesis of a major tightening of risky lending obstructing the AI arms race buildout is increasingly difficult to rebut.

Wall Street analysts have been playing down parallels between "private Credit" and the 2008 crisis. Many cite the lack of leverage; today's well-capitalized banking system with limited "private Credit" exposures; sounder liquidity mechanics (i.e., fund "gates"); and manageable scale ($1.8 to $2.0TN). Fair enough.

Former New York Fed President Bill Dudley wrote a thoughtful piece last week: "Private Credit Is Bad, But Not 2008 Bad." "Will the growing troubles in private credit precipitate a broader financial crisis? As a veteran of the 2008 subprime lending meltdown, I'm extremely hesitant to say no. Nonetheless, I don't think it's likely." And Friday evening from Barron's Randall Forsyth: "Serious but not systemic. That is how to think of the impact on the economy of the ructions in the private-credit market."

"Private Credit" is emerging as a major issue, especially in terms of financing the AI arms race Bubble. That said, my argument is not that high-risk lending is today a paramount systemic risk. Subprime mortgages were certainly not the critical force during 2008's near financial collapse.

Today's risk, as it was during the 2008 crisis, is a major unwind of speculative leverage, marketplace illiquidity, money market instability, derivatives chaos, panic, and market dislocation. A crisis of confidence that commenced at the "periphery" (subprime mortgages) over time penetrated the heart ("core") of perceived safe and liquid (money-like) financial instruments, certainly including Lehman Brothers and Wall Street "repo" and money market obligations.

Repeating a fundamental point, it is sudden fear for perceived safe "money" - as opposed to risky loans/instruments - that is the domain of panics and financial crises.

The MOVE (bond volatility) Index, while down marginally Friday (to 112), traded Thursday at 115, the high since April 25th. The MOVE spiked 30 bps in seven sessions, comparable to the "liberation day" period move. The Investment-grade (VIXIG) Volatility Index surged almost to 61.8 on Friday, the highest since April 11th. Investment-grade CDS jumped 2 to 67.4, the high back to April 23rd.

The VIX (equities volatility) Index jumped 3.5 Friday to 31.05, the highest close since April 21st. High yield CDS spiked 31 bps Friday, the largest upside move since April 10th - to the high since May 1st. High yield spreads blew out 23 Friday to 334 bps - to the widest level since early May.

Major bank CDS rose (2 to 3bps) Friday, with BofA (75bps), Morgan Stanley (80bps), Citigroup (75bps), Goldman Sachs (75bps), Wells Fargo (69bps) and JPMorgan (55bps) all now at or near highs since April. Interestingly, BofA's Global Financial Stress Index (GFSI) ("a gauge that tracks anxiety and stress levels across global financial markets") surpassed "liberation day" levels and traded just below the March 2023 banking mini-crisis peak.

The preponderance of indicators points to a major unfolding de-risking/deleveraging dynamic.

Bitcoin was slammed 4.3% in Friday trading to 66,000, near a one-month low. Bitcoin was down 6.5% for the week, 25% y-t-d, and 47% from October highs. Bitcoin's inability to muster a rally is ominous. The cryptocurrencies, at the speculative risk asset "periphery," set deleveraging in motion. "Risk off" then gravitated to leveraged loans and high yield instruments, certainly including "private Credit." Strengthening contagion effects began to work their way to big tech, a particularly crowded sector (retail, hedge funds, institutions) where major leverage has accumulated over recent years. The precious metals, favored by the levered crowd, also abruptly reversed sharply lower.

The Iran War stoked fledgling risk aversion, though markets were willing to assume a quick end to the conflict. President Trump's repeated declarations of rapid resolution certainly fostered complacency. Another TACO moment came Monday, spurring big, though fleeting, market reversals. Brent crude traded Monday in a 15% intraday range (114.43 to 96.77), only to end the week at 114.57. Brent crude surged 4.2% in Friday trading.

March 25 - Financial Times (Editorial Board): "The damage that could be done to global energy markets by closing the Strait of Hormuz was long anticipated (except, perhaps, in the Oval Office). Less well understood was the fact that the waterway is a vital artery, too, for chemicals, metals and fertilisers. Disruption to shipping is affecting sectors from AI and semiconductors to mining and food production, compounding the energy shock. And, as with energy supplies, upheavals could persist beyond the end of US and Israeli strikes on Iran. Take helium. Qatar accounts for about one-third of global supplies of the gas, a byproduct of natural gas production at the vast Ras Laffan field. Not only are exports blocked through the strait but Ras Laffan's infrastructure has suffered long-lasting damage from Iranian retaliatory strikes. This is bad news for the semiconductor industry, which uses helium to cool wafers during manufacturing; Taiwan and South Korea get the majority of their supplies from Qatar, whose high-purity helium is not easily substituted. Helium is also important for fibre optics, defence manufacturing and medical imaging (the gas cools MRI scanners)."

On many levels, the President's Thursday cabinet meeting was a disturbing spectacle. For a wartime gathering, the President and his team seemed to lack the serious focus deserving of an unfolding national crisis. Many, including global political and business leaders, surely question the President's mental fitness. If we took his comments at face value, we'd conclude the commander-in-chief is delusional: "We don't need Hormuz at all… We have so much oil. Our country is not affected by this."

Industry experts are lining up to pronounce "the worst energy disruption in history." Unprecedented production losses for crude and myriad refined products risk devastating supply chain crises - oil and energy, fertilizer and agriculture, petrochemical and plastics, shipping and logistics - and on and on. A significant global inflationary event is now unfolding, hitting a world where inflation has been running on the hotter side for over five years. It's slamming a highly indebted world. It threatens to destabilize global financial markets exposed to historically unparalleled levels of speculative leverage.

Deleveraging continues to gain momentum in "core" sovereign debt markets, a bastion of Trillions of speculative leverage. Ten-year Treasury yields gained another five bps this week to an eight-week high of 4.43%, with yields now having spiked 49 bps in four weeks. Benchmark MBS yields have surged 73 bps since the start of the war to 5.54%.

Pressure is even more intense in key (highly levered "core") global bond markets. UK 10-year gilt yields traded Friday at an intraday high of 5.10%, the high since July 2, 2008. Thirty-year gilt yields traded up to 5.70%, the high back to May 1998. In just four weeks, UK yields are up 74 bps.

Italian 10-year yields rose as high as 4.14% Friday, and Greek yields to 4.07% - both highs since November 2023. French yields advanced to 3.89% (high since June 2009) and German yields to 3.13% (May 2011). In four weeks, yields have spiked 78 bps in Italy, 72 bps in Greece, 62 bps in France, and 45 bps in Germany.

Indicative of long-term inflation concerns, 30-year German yields rose Friday to 3.57% (high since May 2010) and 30-year French yields to 4.59% (June 2009). Italian and Greek long bond yields rose to 4.82% and 4.65% (both November 2023).

Market vigilantes are not in the mood to give highly indebted Japan a pass. Ten-year JGB yields jumped 11 bps Friday to 2.37% - the high back to February 1999. Thirty-year JGB yields spiked a notable 19 bps Friday to 3.71%, quickly approaching January's spike high to a record 3.88%. As 40-year yields surged 20 bps Friday (3.905%), the dollar/yen traded to 160 for the first time since 2024.

March 26 - Bloomberg (Alexandra Harris): "Hedge funds' borrowing in the repo market has surged 154% since the end of 2022 to $3.1 trillion, reflecting an increase in leveraged-trading strategies, according to the Office of Financial Research. By last June, qualified hedge funds - or those with more than $500 million in assets - were leveraged 2.6 times, meaning they had borrowed $2.60 for every dollar they hold… Some that follow certain strategies - particularly macro, multi-strategy and relative-value funds - were leveraged by 6 times. The hedge funds also financed trades with prime-brokerage loans from securities firms, which increased by 83% to $3 trillion over the same period. Such borrowing can increase the risks in financial markets if hedge funds are forced to unwind money-losing trades or unload other assets to meet demands to post more collateral. Hedge funds' exposure to US Treasuries and related derivatives increased by $1 trillion to $4.1 trillion."

Doing some quick math, hedge funds' combined "repo" and broker loan borrowings surged $3.2 TN since 2002 to $6.1 TN. And this includes only hedge funds obligated to file SEC "form PF." We can assume that a decent chunk of this leverage is related to Treasury "basis trades" and other speculative bond market "spread" trades relatively immune to yield gyrations.

As we witnessed in March 2020, all hell breaks loose when deleveraging, illiquidity, and dislocation engulf the highly levered Treasury "basis trade" marketplace. Markets have not yet reached that point, but they're moving in that direction. Importantly, there are Iran War scenarios that would risk unleashing 2020 and 2008-style deleveraging and panic.

I assume levered hedge funds around the globe are intensively gaming out various war scenarios. I would see the best-case as the U.S. and Iran commencing fraught negotiations, with the U.S. and Israel winding down bombing operations in a couple weeks. After bombings subside, I'll assume it would take some time to get tanker traffic moving again through the Strait. Even under this best-case, we could be at least a month before the Strait of Hormuz begins to meaningfully open up - and additional weeks for the backlog to clear. This would still entail major global dislocations.

The worst-case scenario would see uncontrollable escalation. Iran refuses to bow to U.S. pressure to capitulate in negotiations, while maintaining its tight grip on the Strait of Hormuz. President Trump threatens a massive "final blow" assault. This threat would include destruction of key Iranian energy infrastructure, U.S. troop deployments, U.S. direct control over the Strait of Hormuz, and perhaps even regime change.

The worst-case would see Iran categorically reject Trump's demands, eliciting a major U.S. military response - triggering Iran retaliation against Gulf energy infrastructure and protracted hostilities - including mine laying - around the Strait of Hormuz, along with widening conflict throughout the Gulf region. Interceptor inventories run low. The Houthis enter the war, creating havoc in the Red Sea (Houthis fire first missile of the war tonight into Israel).

I'll loosely refer to a third "base case" scenario, which would see weeks of fraught negotiations and a tailing off of overt hostilities. The President's desire to bring the war to a conclusion puts escalation on hold. Iran talks, but refuses to capitulate, recognizing its capacity to inflict damage to fragile global markets and economies. Iran agrees to allow limited traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, so long as the U.S. and Israel suspend bombings and assassinations. Iran would buy some time, perhaps allowing shipments of drones and supplies from Russia and other replenishments from China. Russia might even surreptitiously promote this scenario to Iranian leadership, support that would place U.S./Russian relations on a collision course. This would drag the conflict out for months - "hot war" to "cold war" to elevated risk for hotter war.

It's difficult for me this evening to envisage high probability for a best-case outcome. Iran doesn't appear poised to capitulate. History argues they won't. They're fighting for survival and playing for time, confident Trump has no appetite for a protracted war, and believes it can exact a heavy price on global markets and Gulf energy infrastructure. The President may gamble that ongoing intense bombings will force capitulation, with Iranian retaliation holding the potential to further destabilize severely impaired energy markets.

Consequences are dire if traffic doesn't get flowing through the Strait of Hormuz ("Strait of Trump"). I assume the major build up in U.S. forces in the Gulf is in preparation for taking control of the Strait. How much capacity Iran has in opposing U.S. intervention is a huge unknown. They've had decades to prepare for such a confrontation. Their firepower has been severely depleted, but they surely retain capacity for asymmetric war tactics.

Gaming this out, I see uncomfortably high probabilities for problematic market outcomes. The leveraged speculating community is surely looking at various scenarios, with the current market risk vs. reward calculus extraordinarily unattractive. For one, the risk of a highly destabilizing scenario is alarmingly high. Furthermore, typical policy responses are not assured. With inflationary risks escalating, the Fed will be slow and initially cautious with QE. Meanwhile, the President and his "put" suffer a credibility crisis.

Ongoing pressure to de-risking/deleverage seems reasonable. That said, powerful short squeezes and hedge unwinds could erupt at any time, providing markets sporadic liquidity boosts. But I expect marketplace liquidity to become an increasingly pressing issue. This week's Treasury auctions warned of waning demand. And it's also worth noting this week's $53 billion drop in money market fund assets and the $33 billion fall in commercial paper borrowings, what I view as indications of nascent fixed income deleveraging.

So far, not much talk of hedge fund losses. Expect things on that front to heat up. There's clearly pain out there and ample impetus to rein in risk and leverage.

Miran added his ideas would need to be 'studied and calibrated', with the implementation process taking 'several years'."

U.S. Economic Bubble Watch:

March 25 - Reuters (Lucia Mutikani): "The U.S. current account deficit narrowed sharply in the fourth quarter, hitting the lowest level in nearly five years, amid a rise in primary income and a reduction in the goods trade deficit partly because of tariffs ‌on imports. The… current account deficit, which measures the flow of goods, services and investments into and out of the country, contracted by $48.4 billion, or 20.2%, to $190.7 billion last quarter."

March 26 - Associated Press (Matt Ott): "The number of Americans applying for jobless aid inched up last week as employers continue to retain workers despite a labor market that has weakened considerably in the past year. U.S. applications for jobless aid for the week ending March 21 rose by 5,000 to 210,000 from the previous week's 205,000… That's right in line with the 210,000 new filings analysts… were expecting… The total number of Americans filing for unemployment benefits for the previous week ending March 14 fell by 32,000 to 1.82 million… That's the lowest number of continuing claims since May 25, 2024…"

March 24 - Axios (Emily Peck): "Workers, especially those with a college degree, think the job market is awful, a new Gallup analysis… finds… Although the unemployment rate is relatively low at the moment, hiring has slowed a lot, particularly for professionals. Deteriorating worker sentiment can signal that things are about to get much worse. 'Workers with higher levels of formal education were markedly less optimistic about the job market in 2025 than those with less schooling,' writes Sarah Fioroni, a senior research consultant at Gallup."

March 26 - Associated Press (Alex Veiga): "The average long-term U.S. mortgage rate climbed this week to its highest level in more than six months… The benchmark 30-year fixed rate mortgage rate rose to 6.38% from 6.22% last week… One year ago, the rate averaged 6.65%. This marks the largest one-week increase since April 2025 and the largest three-week increase since October 2024…"

China Watch:

March 24 - Bloomberg: "China Vanke Co.'s stake in a logistics firm that's facing mounting investor concerns is adding to the strain on the distressed developer, just as another wave of looming debt maturities stokes default risks again. Vanke holds a 21% stake in Singapore-based GLP Pte., which saw its bond prices sink to distressed levels last week."

Central Banker Watch:

March 25 - Wall Street Journal (Paul Hannon): "The European Central Bank would have to respond forcefully if inflation threatened to rise significantly above its target for a long time as a consequence of the conflict in the Middle East, President Christine Lagarde said… However, the policymaker said the key rate wouldn't be lifted until there is 'sufficient' information available to judge the likely impact of the conflict. March 25 - Associated Press (David McHugh): "The head of the European Central Bank says that businesses may be quicker to raise prices in response to the oil shock from the Iran war due to bitter memories of the inflation spike after Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022. If oil and gas prices continue to rise, 'the response of firms and workers may be faster than last time,' ECB President Christine Lagarde said… 'We have a more recent memory of high inflation, which could affect how quickly costs are passed on and compensation is sought,' Lagarde said… 'An entire generation has now lived through its first episode of high inflation - and it may not be as slow to react a second time'."

Europe Watch:

March 25 - Bloomberg (Priscila Azevedo Rocha): "Europe is about to start the gas stockpiling season with key storage tanks depleted, meaning it will need to compete even more with Asian buyers to secure supplies, just as the Middle East conflict disrupts energy flows. Dutch facilities are now just about 6% full, the lowest for this time of year in data going to late 2010… In Germany, home to the region's biggest sites, inventories are also much lower than usual, at about 22%..."

Japan Watch:

March 24 - Bloomberg (Yoshiaki Nohara and Akemi Terukina): "Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi looked to ramp up the nation's response to the fallout from the war in Iran with a thorough review of the ecosystem of oil-related products, as the likelihood builds of shortages and knock-on effects across the economy. The Finance Ministry is also sounding out market participants on possible intervention in the crude oil futures market… in an apparent attempt to ease pressure on the commodity and currency stemming from the crisis."

March 22 - Wall Street Journal (Megumi Fujikawa): "Japanese companies are poised to give workers their most significant pay raise in 35 years… Preliminary data from the nation's largest labor union group, known as Rengo, showed that 1,100 members secured wage increases of 5.26% on average this year. The figure was slightly higher than last year's 5.25% gain and would mark the steepest climb since 1991."

Emerging Market Watch:

March 25 - New York Times (Alex TravelliChoe Sang-HunSui-Lee Wee and Jason Gutierrez): "Across Asia, countries already vulnerable to the sustained disruption in energy supplies from the Persian Gulf are also contending with an ominous side effect. Their currencies are being suffocated by a dollar that is surging in value… The fighting has revealed a second painful choke point. About 90% of international trade in goods - including the oil and gas that are skyrocketing in price - uses the American currency. And as often happens in times of global turmoil, investors are taking money out of riskier regions and putting more money into U.S. assets."

March 22 - Bloomberg (Zijia Song and Vinicius Andrade): "Once a top pick among emerging-market investors, local-currency debt has become a losing trade. After months being boosted by a weaker dollar, slowing inflation and interest-rate cuts, the bonds have delivered a loss of more than 5% since the Iran war started... Just six of the 22 main emerging-market currencies are up against the dollar this year, compared with 17 before the conflict broke out."

March 24 - Bloomberg (Gonzalo Soto and Alex Vasquez): "Mexico's annual inflation accelerated much more than expected in early March… Inflation quickened to 4.63% in the first two weeks of March compared to the same period last year… The reading was… well over late February's 4.13% print."

March 24 - Bloomberg (Martha Viotti Beck): "Brazil's central bank warned that inflation risks intensified after war in the Middle East broke out and said future steps in its easing cycle will be determined as new information is incorporated… 'The risks to the inflation scenarios, both to the upside and to the downside, which were already higher than usual, intensified after the beginning of the Middle East conflicts,' they said…"

March 25 - Bloomberg (Andy Mukherjee): "India's largest private-sector lender, HDFC Bank Ltd., is scrambling to respond to investors' concerns that highlight the extent to which some of the country's most esteemed firms have lost precious cachet - and, occasionally, the market's faith. Chairman Atanu Chakraborty resigned March 18, citing 'certain happenings and practices' at the bank that are 'not in congruence with my personal Values and Ethics.' He later told a TV interviewer that he didn't depart due to wrongdoing at the lender."

Social, Political, Environmental and Cybersecurity Instability Watch:

March 25 - Associated Press (Tammy Webber): "Oil depots spewing black smoke. Debris sinking in the Persian Gulf. Missiles pounding military sites. The Iran war has unleashed a toxic mix of chemicals, heavy metals and other pollutants that threaten everything from agriculture to drinking water to people's health - and will leave behind environmental damage and health risks that could persist for decades, experts said. 'All the burning of oil and gas fields in the coastal areas, all the ships that are there, the oil tankers that are being burned or (sunk) - all of these mean pollution,' said Kaveh Madani, an Iranian scientist and director of the United Nations University Institute for Water, Environment and Health."

March 24 - Associated Press (Seth Borenstein): "After smashing March heat records in 14 states and the U.S. as a whole, the gigantic heat dome that's baked the Southwest is creeping eastward and may end up being one of the most expansive heat waves in American history, meteorologists and weather historians said. And it's not going away for a while, maybe not till the middle of the next week as April starts, said meteorologist Gregg Gallina of the National Weather Service's Weather Prediction Center. 'Basically the entire U.S. is going to be hot,' Gallina said... 'The area of record temperatures is extremely large. That's the thing that's really bizarre.' This heat dome - in which high pressure is acting like a pot lid trapping hot air over a region - will leave Flagstaff, Arizona, with 11 or 12 straight days of temperatures higher than the city's previous March record…"

March 24 - Bloomberg (Alastair Marsh): "JPMorgan… says aging, run-down grid infrastructure now risks undermining security goals, with everything from extreme weather to cyberattacks posing a growing threat. The Wall Street bank… describes the current decades-old grid network as a 'national security risk.' Against that backdrop, investments that make grid infrastructure more resilient are becoming 'increasingly attractive,' according to JPMorgan."

March 23 - Bloomberg (Aaron Clark): "Global air quality declined in 2025, with more cities reporting standards below international health guidelines on the impact of severe wildfires and pollution from sectors including fossil fuels and agriculture. Data from almost 9,500 cities showed 14% met World Health Organization standards for annual average concentrations of harmful fine-particle matter, or PM2.5, according to… IQAir Group. That compared to a total of 17% a year earlier when nearly 9,000 cities were surveyed."

March 26 - Bloomberg (Brian Kahn and Eric Roston): "Sea ice in the Arctic has tied last year's record for its lowest-ever winter coverage. The findings, published… by the US National Snow and Ice Data Center, add to the clear signals that the Arctic is undergoing a rapid shift as the fastest-warming part of the world. Last year, sea ice topped out at roughly 14.3 million square kilometers. That represented a loss of 2.3 million square kilometers of ice… compared to 1979… This year the winter ice reached its high point on March 14, at a level that's virtually indistinguishable from the year before."

March 22 - Financial Times (Emiliya Mychasuk): "The Earth's energy imbalance reached record levels last year, as the rate of solar radiation that entered the planet exceeded the amount leaving the system at a faster rate, the World Meteorological Organization said. The measure was included for the first time in the UN agency's State of the Climate annual report, as the rate had more than doubled in the past 20 years while greenhouse gases continued to accumulate."

data.title: DouYu: Worth The Risk At Far Below Cash On The Books
data.description:
DouYu (DOYU), a live streaming platform in China for games and e-sports, is off to a horrible 2026 with the stock having lost 31.5% YTD, and that is with a bounce after the release of the Q4 FY2025 report on March 25. However, DOYU has a shot at adding to the recent bounce for a number of reasons, especially if DOYU can maintain what it showed in the latest report.

DOYU has sold off, but a bullish signal may herald better times

A previous article from November 2025 written by myself mentioned a number of issues that needed to be addressed by DOYU, which included declining revenue and a decrease in monthly active users, or MAUs, both direct consequences of a downturn in the market for live streaming in China. On the other hand, DOYU was working on addressing its faults and making some progress in certain areas, which gave reason for optimism that DOYU could fix whatever that needed to be addressed. Accordingly, I chose to rate DOYU a buy in the article.

For a while it looked like DOYU had managed to stop the large decline in the stock that started in July 2025, as it headed into the new year having gone sideways for several months, but 2026 saw a resumption of the decline in the stock. The chart above shows how the stock fell in the first couple of months to hit a 2026 low of $4.28 per ADS on February 24, although it closed the day significantly higher at $4.48.

However, what follows next could be viewed as a bullish signal for longs. Note how in the chart above, the bounce following the February 24 low was succeeded by another move downwards that resulted in DOYU almost matching the February 24 low on March 24. The stock hit an intraday low of $4.50 per ADS on March 24, but closed the day somewhat higher at $4.53. All this is significant for multiple reasons.

First, the lows are exactly one month apart. On a closing basis, the February 24 low of $4.48 and the March 24 low of $4.53, are just $0.05 apart, or roughly one percent of each other. The fact that the March 24 low is somewhat higher is also worth noting. All this points in the direction of a double bottom, which is considered a bullish reversal chart pattern, especially since it follows in the wake of a long decline that started with the July 2025 high of $9.34 per ADS.

If this is correct, and the stock is heading north after reversing course, then DOYU may be able to move unopposed until the first potential resistance level. This could be the 23.6% Fibonacci retracement of the preceding move downwards, or the July 2025 high of $9.34 to the February 2026 low of $4.28, which is $5.47.

If DOYU manages to get past the first hurdle at $5.47, DOYU may be able to go as high as the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement level at $7.41 per ADS, before resistance is likely to offer its strongest challenge to prevent DOYU from moving higher. But to get there, DOYU would first need to get past the 38.2% and 50% Fibonacci retracement levels, which are at $6.21 and $6.81, respectively.

What could drive DOYU higher

While DOYU may have sent a bullish signal, that alone may not be enough to convince everyone that DOYU is worth pursuing. Some may need more to get on board. It just so happens that there are other catalysts that could power a move higher by DOYU. But before we go into that, it is necessary to look at what caused DOYU to fall off in recent years, since that would help explain how it can get out of the hole.

DOYU is a live streaming platform in China, which greatly benefited when COVID-19 broke out and the government in China resorted to lockdowns. This is because it removed a number of potential avenues for entertainment, especially those that required large numbers of people in close proximity to each other, which opened opportunities for live streaming. More people would turn to live streaming because it was compatible with people staying in the confines of their homes during the pandemic.

DOYU grew greatly during the pandemic years, but the reverse happened when the lockdowns were lifted and people could go back to doing all the things they used to do before the pandemic. Live streaming saw a reduction in the number of users and/or the time spent on the platform, which naturally affected DOYU. A slower economy meant people were less willing to spend. Adding to the pressure were new regulations in China that sought to combat some of the problems on live streaming platforms.

This included, for instance, some influencers using the live streaming platform to make misleading or even false claims in order to promote certain products and/or services. China has also sought to regulate the practice of tipping or other forms of rewards during live streaming. In short, action was taken to curb various issues on live streaming platforms that were deemed problematic, all of which impacted DOYU.

DOYU seems to be recovering, according to the latest report

The live streaming industry underwent a downturn as a result of the above and other reasons, which also made its way to DOYU. DOYU saw both the top and the bottom line shrink compared to the heady days of the pandemic around 2021 or so. However, recent reports show DOYU is recovering as quarterly results have significantly improved.

In the most recent or Q4 FY2025 report, released on March 25, FY2025 revenue decreased to RMB3,818.85M, or $546.09M, less than RMB4,270.83M in FY2024, but DOYU was able to reduce its GAAP net loss attributable to shareholders to RMB29.08M, or $4.16M, which is much less than the net loss of RMB306.81M a year ago.

The results look even better when only the core business is considered. Income from operations actually got out of the red with RMB4.76M, or $0.68M, in FY2025, better than the loss of RMB573.59M in FY2024. If not for impairment charges, which pushed the net results into the red, the numbers could have looked even better.

Per share, the loss of RMB0.96 or $0.14 per ADS in FY2025 is better than the loss of RMB9.95 in FY2024. In terms of non-GAAP, which leaves out a number of items like impairment charges, DOYU reported net income of RMB40.16M, or $5.74M, in FY2025, better than the net loss of RMB249.23M in FY2024. This translates to a net income of RMB1.33 or $0.19 per ADS in FY2025, up from a net loss of RMB8.08 in FY2024.

Long DOYU can count on a strong balance sheet

The improvement in the income statement has implications for the balance sheet. DOYU has a very strong balance sheet, but as long as the income statement is in the red, one has to account for the possibility the balance sheet will be negatively affected by the income statement. But if the income statement is no longer a problem, or something close to it, then that lifts a weight off of the balance sheet.

This is important because DOYU has a low valuation in comparison to its balance sheet. DOYU ended FY2025 with cash and cash equivalents of $251.55M, which by itself far exceeds DOYU’s current market cap of $141M. There is also another $71.86M in short-term bank deposits, which means DOYU is valued at less than half the net cash, or what can be converted to cash, on the books since total debt stands at just $1.4M. This does not include another $54.87M DOYU has in long-term investments.

As far as the balance sheet is concerned, DOYU has a book value of $9.33 per ADS, which means DOYU is valued at a price-to-book of just 0.5x, with the stock last closing at $4.66 per ADS on March 27. If we assume DOYU will no longer spend time in the red since both operating income and non-GAAP net income were positive and GAAP income would have been if not for impairment charges, then DOYU could potentially be worth twice what it currently goes for.

What DOYU needs to do to clear doubts

However, if DOYU wants to get back to what it is supposed to be worth according to book value, if not higher, it needs to remove all doubts that it is at risk of incurring net losses since that can reduce book value if left to its devices. DOYU did show progress by getting out of the red in terms of most metrics, but it is still quite close to falling back into the red to take it easy.

For instance, income from operations turned positive after eliminating the big losses from before, but at just $0.68M, it would not take much for DOYU to fall back into the red. The main issue for DOYU is that the top line continues to shrink, even though the bottom line has gotten better. While DOYU managed to end in the black in pretty much everything in the last or Q4 FY2025 quarter, including non-GAAP and GAAP net income and operating income, revenue still contracted.

In Q4 FY2025, revenue decreased from RMB1,136.0M a year ago to RMB918.78M, or $131.38M, even though it reported income from operations of $675,000, GAAP net income of $196,000 or $0.01 per ADS, and non-GAAP net income of $1.8M, or $0.06 per ADS, all much better than one year ago. This continued contraction could create doubt in the minds of potential buyers, as it leaves open the possibility DOYU could regress and fall back into the red in terms of the income statement. DOYU will need to show it can grow revenue sustainably if it does not want to be discounted based on its balance sheet.

The silver lining here is that DOYU may be doing better in terms of revenue than the headline numbers suggest it is. DOYU has become more selective in terms of what live streaming events to host by focusing less on those where returns are lower in terms of profitability. While these events may bring in revenue, they add little to the bottom line and may even be subtracting from it.

Eliminating them helps DOYU’s profitability, but it also means less revenue coming in. The flip side is that once DOYU has weeded out all the low performers, the negative impact on the top line should go away. Once this happens, revenue growth could return, which would work well with an improved bottom line.

Investor takeaways

DOYU has gone through a period in which the company struggled to adapt to a new environment after the boom during the pandemic. However, there are signs DOYU is getting back on track. Income from operations turned positive in FY2025, as did non-GAAP net income. In terms of GAAP, DOYU was still in the red, although impairment charges were a factor, but it is good to see how DOYU managed to improve on this in the final or Q4 FY2025 quarterly report with a GAAP profit. All in all, the recently released report is consistent with a company on the upswing.

This is relevant in relation to the balance sheet. DOYU is currently available at less than half the net cash, or what can be converted to cash, on the books. This could be justified if there was reason to believe book value could go down due to net losses, but this argument becomes harder to make if the income statement is improving to the point there are no more losses.

The case for long DOYU has another argument in its favor with the stock posting what looks like a double bottom chart pattern. This could signal a reversal in the stock, which suggests the stock is heading higher after going down for some time. Combine it with what is going on in the income statement and the balance sheet, and long DOYU is at the very least not without some merit.

DOYU does need to show it can grow the top line, but while the headline numbers have yet to show this, a closer look at what is going on at DOYU gives reason to believe that this too is something DOYU can achieve. In any case, when valuations are so low that DOYU is valued at less than half net cash, it becomes easier to forgive DOYU for a flaw it has yet to get rid of.

I am thus bullish on DOYU all things considered. There is the risk that the bottom line could regress due to a contraction in the top line, but at less than half the net cash, DOYU is worth the risk. DOYU has been able to greatly improve the most pressing issue, profitability, and it deserves a shot at showing it can solve the other problem of lower revenue. If DOYU can be considered a profitable business, then it should not be worth much less than the cash it has access to, like it is right now. Chances are this won’t remain the case for long.

data.title: Buda Juice: The Optionality Isn't Mine
data.description:
Some say Buddha spent 42 whole days meditating under the Bodhi tree. Well, my coverage of Buda Juice (BUDA) is a little over that. About three months.

But, to tell the truth, my 'Sell' call here hasn't reached enlightenment yet. It delivered ~4% while the S&P fell just over 7%.

Funny that I followed the post-earnings reaction. It wasn't good at all. I don't know if IPO investors expected faster post-IPO growth or something like that.

The stock started to slip in the high single digits, but recovered in the middle of the session and went flat.

The pre-market view isn't very pretty.

Right now it's down ~5%. You know how things are. In a stock with such low float, that means practically nothing.

Just as a reminder, the Buda Juice thesis is really about the scale Country Fresh (Brian Herr's company and Buda's second-largest shareholder) could bring. It gains access to proprietary UltraFresh tech, while Buda taps into nationwide distribution.

It's very good for Herr, but I have my doubts about whether it's really good for the minority shareholder at these multiples.

You would be paying ~8x sales for the acceleration of the distribution curve (Country Fresh will accelerate this) and, consequently, the risk of executing the expansion. But, looking at the other side, you would also be paying for an optionality that isn't yours.

Who will guarantee that there won't be value leakage for the minority shareholder?

Buda Juice's CEO sold some shares in the IPO, and if Herr becomes the majority shareholder, he could (and it would even be the most understandable thing to do) prioritize Country Fresh.

There are many 'whys' on the table. And, to keep things realistic, I'll give a peek at the quarter.

Nothing New Under The Sun

To start off, if I'm basing this on the material management provided to us at the IPO, Buda has a 38,000 sq ft production plant.

And that was expected. The proceeds from the IPO (~$20 million) are expected to be allocated to two new projects (one in South Carolina and another expanding the existing one on the West Coast).

The Dallas plant is probably already running. This would give them a maximum revenue capacity of $55 million today.

As they pulled $12.6 million in all of FY 2025, I imagine they are operating at only 22.9% of their current production capacity.

Well, I expected something like that.

In fact, I even expected a slightly bigger bump. My projections were targeting $19.5 million in the base scenario.

Regarding capacity, I also imagined something similar. Buda juices (like all organic juices) have a short shelf life. This is a model where you simply can't have overcapacity if you don't want to see your inventory spoiled.

Did the market want to see stronger growth this quarter? I really don't know, but I suspect so, given the post-earnings reaction.

Gross margin also contracted slightly. It fell from 46.2% to 44.4%.

It's not something you should worry too much about, as the CEO told us it was due to some increase in the price of citrus fruits. I guess I should get used to stable/slightly declining gross margins for FY 2026/2027.

Add to that higher SG&A due to the IPO, and you'll see a slightly lower EBITDA margin as well. As I said, it's nothing to worry about.

But right now it doesn't look like a company that should trade at 8x sales.

Here's a peek at the margins:

The good news is that they remain larger than peers like Vita Coco (COCO) and should maintain their structural size as they expand.

Of course, there's the typical problem of any cold chain when expanding. But, at the level I'm seeing here, the EBIT margin would still be quite healthy. Horatio Lonsdale-Hands, the CEO of Buda Juice, rightly likes to say that this is a profitable small-cap since its IPO.

If I earned a dollar for every company that's just another 'empty promise' during its IPO, I'd be rich by now. That's not the case here.

Buda generated $3.2 million in OCF in the quarter. Almost all of that ($3 million, to be more precise) became FCF given the very low Capex. It's still an FCF yield of just over 2.3%, but the idea is that this will increase as they execute the plan.

$55 Billion? I Don't Think So

Taking advantage of the fact that Buda Juice is a company that sells healthy products, it's time for me to try and ask some healthy questions.

The first one has to do with the TAM thesis they are aiming for going forward.

For example, the company's materials estimate a market of ~$55 billion in 2024 with no 'true fresh juice on the market.' By 2033 they expect an even larger market ($77.5 billion) and the 'true fresh juice leader' throne vacant.

From what I understand, the idea is for Buda Juice to reclaim this throne.

But I think this runs into some exaggerations.

The first is that there is no other 'true fresh' juice and that consumers would really see Buda Juice as part of a category apart from other juices out there.

Most folks will just buy the fruit and juice it at home.

Demand here depends on both Gen-Z and health-conscious Millennials. And, taking a peek at consumer confidence, you can see that despite these being the most confident generations, they seem to be cutting back on fitness expenses here and there.

It seems to me that the achievable TAM is lower. I won't guess a specific number, but I imagine something up to ~50% lower and perhaps a bit volatile since there's built-in elasticity here.

Adjusting Some Projections

For Q1 FY 2026, the CFO told us on the earnings call that revenue was growing at mid-teens year-over-year.

So, I'm aiming for something close to $3.8 to $3.9 million in revenue. I know it's quite difficult, but if we annualize that growth, FY 2026 would deliver a total revenue of almost $15.5 million.

But there are some caveats here.

The table I showed you earlier shows a total capacity of $110 million after the East Coast plant opens. That's 14% utilization of total capacity. I'll use this as a possible base scenario.

As a bull, I can project that they maintain current utilization (22.9%) and reach $25.2 million. Almost 100% year-over-year growth.

It seems risky, but perhaps a ramp-up from Country Fresh could deliver that right away.

If FY 2026 is a mystery, FY 2027 is even more.

If we follow what the chart actually showed, capacity at the end of the year would reach $165 million, ~$37.8 million based on current capacity utilization.

Will it be possible to deliver a CAGR of over 30%? Well, again, I think so, but only if Country Fresh does the heavy lifting.

But maybe I'm being too conservative using that capacity, right? I'll stick with my FY 2026 scenarios, and I want you to tell me your predictions in the comments.

The more viewpoints, the better.

Now, regarding the EBITDA margin, I'm going to consider it slightly lower. Here I'm modeling the impact of slightly more expensive citrus fruits and some inefficiencies when they expand the cold chain to the East Coast.

So, if they pulled ~29%, I imagine something closer to 27%.

We're talking about an EBITDA of $4.1 to $6.8 million. It's a huge range, but it's justifiable given all the uncertainty here.

Taking Vita Coco's EV/EBITDA (~21.8x) and considering that $5 of the $20 million they scooped up in the IPO will be used for the East Coast plant, I arrive at a price target of $6.50 to $10.70 for the base and bull scenarios, respectively.

Remember that for the bull scenario to happen, they would need to double their revenue by FY 2026.

If I take a weighted average where this would have a ~20% chance of happening, the price target here would be just over $7.50.

Pretty close to what I found in the last article, don't you think?

The best thing to do (in my view, of course) is to stay out of this IPO and observe from the sidelines.

Sales growing in the low teens isn't what minority investors would like to see with the Dallas plant already operational. I imagine this could start scaling at the end of this year when the East Coast plant opens.

With these fat margins, a soaring revenue (remember that for upside above $10 per share it would almost need to double) would be a good trigger for a 'Buy' here.

Buda Juice still seems to me more like a cold chain company, which is a great driver for Country Fresh. Anything beyond that is quite speculative, and, precisely because it's a well-managed company, it's trading at sky-high multiples.

data.title: Whale's Insight: A Macro-Driven Market With No Safe Haven, And No End To Volatility
data.description:
This week, Trump's flip-flopping triggered three major market reversals in five days as gold, equities, and crypto fell in unison with no safe haven in sight. Multiple scenarios are taking shape depending on how long the Hormuz closure lasts and where the conflict heads. On-chain commodity perpetuals are booming as tradfi migrates to 24/7 venues, while institutional ETF flows are fading on both BTC (BTC-USD) and ETH (ETH-USD).

Three Scenarios for a Macro-Driven, Volatile Market

Trump's Flip-Flopping Is the Largest Source of Market Volatility

This week, Trump's media posts triggered three major directional reversals across global markets in under five days, producing over $1 billion in crypto liquidations alone.

  • Mar 22 (Sat): Trump demands Iran reopen the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours or face destruction of its power plants. BTC drops over the weekend. $400M+ in crypto futures liquidated, 85% longs.

  • Mar 23 (Sun): Iran rejects the ultimatum, threatens to destroy Gulf energy infrastructure. Oil futures gap higher; Brent briefly touches $119.

  • Mar 24 (Mon): Trump TACOs. Announces a 5-day pause on strikes, citing "productive conversations" with Tehran. Markets explode in the opposite direction. Oil crashes 14% and S&P futures swing from -1% to +3%. This time it is the shorts getting wiped: $383M in crypto shorts liquidated in one hour.

  • Mar 26 (Thu): Conflict re-escalates. Oil surges (Brent +5.7%), and the S&P drops 1.74%, its worst single day of 2026. After market close, Trump extends the deadline again to April 6. Second reversal in five days.

BTC was whipsawed between $67K and $71K, a $4,000 range driven entirely by geopolitical headlines. The violent wicks in both directions produced over $1 billion in total cryptocurrency liquidations across the week.

Hormuz Scenarios: What Happens If This Drags On

The real substance behind all the market volatility is this: roughly 20% of global oil and gas transits through the Strait of Hormuz, and that waterway has been effectively shut for four weeks.

The IEA chief called the current situation worse than the combined 1973 and 1979 oil crises.

We currently sit between "partial disruption" and "full closure, 30 days." Trump's April 6 extension guarantees at least 10 more days of uncertainty.

If Hormuz stays shut past mid-April, the market reprices toward $150+ oil and a global recession scenario.

Cross-Asset Fallout

Crypto is being driven entirely by macro. BTC sits at $69–70K, down 46% from its ATH, and the broader crypto market is down 21% YTD. There are no crypto-native catalysts for the decline.

The transmission chain is clear: oil spike → inflation expectations rise → Fed stays hawkish → dollar strengthens → all non-yielding risk assets get sold.

The derivatives market confirms the bearish positioning: put/call open interest ratio has reached 0.84, the highest since June 2021; funding rates are negative across most altcoins; and CryptoQuant warns that structural bottom signals have not appeared.

When a market ignores all good news, it is telling you that the macro overhang must clear before anything else can matter.

Gold has fallen 22% from its $5,602 ATH to $4,373, with last week's 9.6% drop the worst since 2011. Nine consecutive down days.

Gold crashing during a war is the most counterintuitive signal of this cycle. It tells you the market fears the inflation-to-rate-hike-to-recession chain reaction more than the war itself.

S&P 500 posted its worst day of 2026 on March 26 (-1.74%). Russell 2000 entered correction territory on March 20. Four consecutive weekly declines. Energy is the only sector in the green.

Beneficiaries: Oil, energy equities, tanker stocks, defense stocks, and fertilizer producers. The U.S. dollar is strengthening as the sole safe haven, punishing all EM currencies and net energy importers.

What Comes Next: Scenarios That Will Shape Every Asset

March 27 presents a rare catalyst convergence on a single day:

  1. U.S. February Core PCE release, the Fed's preferred inflation measure. Early estimates point to a hot print, with Capital Economics projecting core PCE at 0.42% MoM, which would keep the YoY rate pinned near 3.1%, well above the Fed's 2% target. This number will determine whether the "higher for longer" narrative strengthens further or begins to soften.

  2. A $14.16B Bitcoin options expiry on Deribit, with max pain at $75K, roughly 8% above current spot price.

Beyond today, the trajectory of markets depends on two variables above all else: the duration of the Hormuz closure and the direction of the war. Below are the three most probable scenarios and their cross-asset implications.

Scenario A: De-escalation Within Weeks (TACO Base Case)

Trump reaches some form of face-saving deal before or around the April 6 deadline. Hormuz reopens partially. Oil falls back toward $85–90.

  • Crypto: Sharp relief rally. BTC likely retests $75K–80K as the macro overhang lifts and ETF inflows resume at pre-FOMC pace. Altcoins with strong fundamentals (SOL, ETH) could see outsized bounces given how compressed positioning is.

  • Gold: Rebounds toward $4,800–5,000 as real rate pressure eases and the structural bull case (central bank buying, de-dollarization) reasserts.

  • Equities: Quick recovery. Russell 2000 exits correction territory. Tech leads. Defense and energy give back gains.

Scenario B: Prolonged Stalemate (1–3 Months)

No deal. Hormuz remains partially disrupted. Oil grinds between $100 and $150. Military operations continue at current intensity.

  • Crypto: Continues trading as a macro hostage. BTC range-bound between $60K and $72K, with periodic wicks driven by Trump posts and war headlines. Institutional ETF flows stay mixed and low-conviction.

  • Gold: Finds a floor in the $4,200-4,500 range as paper liquidation exhausts itself, then slowly recovers. Physical premiums remain elevated.

  • Equities: Grind lower. Recession probability rises. The Atlanta Fed GDPNow, already at 2.3%, could approach zero or turn negative. EM equities under severe pressure from dollar strength and energy costs.

Scenario C: Full Escalation (Tail Risk)

Hormuz shuts completely for 3+ months. The conflict widens (Hezbollah, Houthis). Oil surges past $200. Central banks are paralyzed.

  • Crypto: COVID-style broad risk-off. BTC could retest $50K–55K as institutional capital exits all risk assets. However, ETF structural demand and the $70K short-term holder cost basis provide a harder floor than previous cycles. A capitulation flush followed by aggressive institutional re-entry is the pattern to watch.

  • Gold: After the initial paper liquidation phase, gold re-emerges as the ultimate safe haven. A move toward $6,000+ becomes plausible as fiat confidence erodes.

  • Equities: Bear market. S&P 500 decline of 20%+ from current levels. Energy and defense are the only hedges. Dollar strengthens aggressively, crushing EM currencies and triggering capital flight.

The Variable That Matters Most

Duration is everything. A 2-week resolution is a buying opportunity across every asset class. A 6-month closure rewrites the macro playbook entirely. The market cannot price this binary outcome, which is exactly why volatility remains extreme.

Trump's April 6 deadline provides an uncertain window of calm, but ground-level military escalation continues regardless of diplomatic signals. U.S.-Iran peace talks remain highly uncertain. As long as neither side can reach a compromise that aligns their core interests, the conflict will continue to drain markets. That said, Trump could TACO at any moment, and the Strait of Hormuz could reopen just as suddenly.

In a market defined by this level of unpredictability, investors should approach every position with caution.

Commodities Now Dominate Perpetual Futures

Traditional financial assets are increasingly being traded on-chain through perpetual futures, and the growth is accelerating. Aggregate open interest across permissionless perpetual markets reached an all-time high of $1.74 billion on March 23, up 25% in a single week.

Commodity-linked perpetuals now account for over 67% of permissionless perpetual futures activity in Q1 2026, up from near zero just six months ago.

On March 23, on-chain commodity perpetual markets hit a record single-day volume of $5.4 billion. The breakdown: silver $1.3B, WTI crude $1.2B, Brent crude $940M and gold $558M. Oil and silver perps generated more volume than SOL and XRP combined that day.

The chart below shows HIP-3 markets by asset category.

Oil-linked perpetual contracts have hit $1.7 billion in peak daily volume with roughly $300 million in open interest. The driver is simple: when CME closes for the weekend, on-chain oil perps are the only venue for real-time price discovery.

Weekend trading activity has surged 9x since January. The chart below shows HIP-3 weekend trading volume (USD).

Macro risk no longer waits for market hours. On-chain perpetuals have emerged as a 24/7 global barometer for geopolitical and commodity risk, blurring the line between crypto infrastructure and traditional derivatives markets.

Market Flow: Institutional Money Is Hesitating

BTC Spot ETF: Four consecutive weeks of net inflows have been broken. The FOMC's hawkish signal on March 18, combined with escalating war uncertainty, killed the momentum that had been building since early March.

ETH Spot ETF: The picture is worse. Not only was the six-day inflow streak terminated, but flows have been net negative since March 18, draining approximately $1.1 billion in total.

Institutional capital is hesitating again. The combination of a hawkish Fed, surging oil, and unpredictable war headlines has pushed institutions back to the sidelines.

ETF inflows have been the only structural source of buying since January. When that flow dries up, there is simply no incremental capital entering the market in the short term.

Week Ahead

  • Ongoing: Iran war enters Week 5. The Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed. Ground-level escalation is now the primary focus, and any ceasefire signal could end the conflict rapidly.

  • Mar 31: Eurozone Core CPI Flash Estimate (YoY)

  • Apr 1: US ISM Manufacturing PMI (March)

  • Apr 6: Trump's extended deadline for Iran to reach a deal or face strikes on energy infrastructure

The war and the April 6 deadline define the window. Whether Trump TACOs again, escalates, or reaches an actual deal will set the direction for oil, and oil will set the direction for everything else.

Meanwhile, the CPI and PMI releases will show whether the oil shock is already feeding into real economic damage. If European inflation runs hot and U.S. manufacturing contracts, the stagflation narrative hardens further, leaving central banks with even less room to act.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.
